does: This paper develops a system that uses large language models (ChatGPT) integrated with a humanoid robot (Pepper) to generate natural and expressive co-speech gestures in real time. The core contribution combines LLM-based code generation for gesture synthesis with reinforcement learning trained on human feedback to iteratively improve gesture quality. Rather than relying on pre-authored animations or traditional machine learning that struggles with naturalness at scale, the system leverages LLMs to dynamically generate gesture trajectories from natural language descriptions, then refines these outputs through a human-in-the-loop RL process that learns what gestures humans perceive as natural and contextually appropriate.
connects: This work builds on several converging research threads: gesture generation systems for social robots that traditionally relied on hand-crafted animations or limited learned models, recent advances in LLM-based code generation that enable runtime synthesis of complex behaviors, and human-feedback-based reinforcement learning methods like RLHF that have proven effective for aligning AI systems with human preferences. It addresses a long-standing challenge in human-robot interaction where robots struggle to produce socially appropriate, expressive movements that complement speech naturally, extending beyond prior work that either used rigid pre-programmed gestures or learned models constrained by small action spaces and limited naturalness perception.
enables: This approach opens pathways for more flexible and responsive social robot behavior across diverse conversational contexts without requiring extensive animation libraries or retraining. It demonstrates how LLMs can serve as flexible action generators for embodied AI systems, potentially extending beyond gestures to other expressive robot behaviors. The work suggests future directions in scaling human feedback collection for robot learning, exploring how LLM-based generation might handle more complex multimodal behaviors, and developing methods to ensure robot expressiveness generalizes across different cultural contexts and social norms, ultimately improving long-term human acceptance and naturalistic interaction in real-world deployment scenarios.
